name: fixinventorylib

metadata.name: fixinventorylib
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: GNU Affero General Public License v3 or later (AGPLv3+)

documentation:
# `fixlib`
Fix Inventory common library


## Table of contents

* [Overview](#overview)
* [Contact](#contact)
* [License](#license)


## Overview
This is the Fix Inventory common library. Any functionality that is required by more than one of [our components](https://github.com/someengineering/fixinventory#component-list) will be put in here.

## Contact
If you have any questions feel free to [join our Discord](https://discord.gg/fixsecurity) or [open a GitHub issue](https://github.com/someengineering/fixinventory/issues/new).


## License
See [LICENSE](../LICENSE) for details.

functions.name: fixlib.json_bender.bend
documentation:
The main bending function.

mapping: the map of benders
source: a dict to be bent

returns a new dict according to the provided map.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.Bender
documentation: Base bending class.
General idea and basic implementation is taken from: https://github.com/Onyo/jsonbender

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.S
documentation: Retrieve a value from a JSON object under given path.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.K
documentation: Selects a constant value.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.F
documentation:
Lifts a python callable into a Bender, so it can be composed.
The extra positional and named parameters are passed to the function at
bending time after the given value.

Example:
```
f = F(sorted, key=lambda d: d['id'])
K([{'id': 3}, {'id': 1}]) >> f  #  -> [{'id': 1}, {'id': 3}]
```

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.GetItem
documentation:
Can be applied to a list or dict bender via `[index]`.
List: GetItem(0) -> first element
Dictionary: GetItem('key') -> value of key

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.Compose
documentation:
Compose two benders.
Use `>>` instead of calling `Compose` directly.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.UnaryOperator
documentation:
Base class for unary bending operators.

Whenever a unary op is activated, the op() method is called with the
*value* (that is, the bender is implicitly activated).

Subclasses must implement the op() method, which takes one value and
should return the desired result.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.BinaryOperator
documentation:
Base class for binary bending operators.

Whenever a bin op is activated, the op() method is called with both
*values* (that is, the benders are implicitly activated).

Subclasses must implement the op() method, which takes two values and
should return the desired result.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.Sort
documentation: Sort a list based on given extractor bender.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.Sorted
documentation: Sort a JSON element

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.AsDate
documentation:
Parse a given input string as date.
The format of the date needs to be defined.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.AsDateString
documentation:
Parse a given input timestamp as date string.
The format of the date needs to be defined.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.Forall

methods.name: fixlib.json_bender.Forall.bend
documentation:
Return a ForallBend instance that bends each element of the list with the
given mapping.

mapping: a JSONBender mapping as passed to the `bend()` function.
context: optional. the context that will be passed to `bend()`.
         Note that if context is not passed, it defaults at bend-time
         to the one passed to the outer mapping.

Example:
```
source = [{'a': 23}, {'a': 27}]
bender = Forall.bend({'b': S('a')})
bender(source)  # -> [{'b': 23}, {'b': 27}]
```

Similar to Python's map().
Builds a new list by applying the given function to each element of the
iterable.

Example:
```
Forall(lambda i: i * 2)(range(5))  # -> [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]
```

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.ForallBend
documentation:
Bends each element of the list with given mapping and context.

mapping: a JSONBender mapping as passed to the `bend()` function.
context: optional. the context that will be passed to `bend()`.
         Note that if context is not passed, it defaults at bend-time
         to the one passed to the outer mapping.

classes.name: fixlib.json_bender.MapDict
documentation: If you have a dict and want to map either key or value.

classes.name: fixlib.threading.ExecutorQueue
documentation:
Use an underlying executor to perform work in parallel, but limit the number of tasks per key.
If fail_on_first_exception_in_group is True, then the first exception in a group
will not execute any more tasks in the same group.

functions.name: fixlib.json.value_in_path
documentation:
Access a value in a json object by a defined path.
{"a": {"b": {"c": 1}}} -> value_in_path({"a": {"b": {"c": 1}}}, ["a", "b", "c"]) -> 1
The path can be defined as a list of strings or as a string with dots as separator.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.key_from_psk

Derive a 256 bit key from a passphrase/pre-shared-key.
A salt can be optionally provided. If not one will be generated.
Returns both the key and the salt.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.encode_jwt
documentation: Encodes a payload into a JWT either using a pre-shared-key or an RSA private key.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.encode_jwt_pki
documentation: Encodes a payload into a JWT either using an RSA private key.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.encode_jwt_psk
documentation:
Encodes a payload into a JWT and signs using a key derived from a pre-shared-key.
Stores the key's salt in the JWT headers.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.decode_jwt
documentation:
Decode a JWT using a key derived from a pre-shared-key and a salt stored
in the JWT headers or an RSA public key.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.decode_jwt_psk
documentation:
Decode a JWT using a key derived from a pre-shared-key and a salt stored
in the JWT headers.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.decode_jwt_pki
documentation: Decode a JWT using an RSA public key.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.encode_jwt_to_headers
documentation:
Takes a payload and psk turns them into a JWT and adds that to a http headers
dictionary. Also returns that dict.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.decode_jwt_from_headers
documentation:
Retrieves the Authorization header from a http headers dictionary and
passes it to `decode_jwt_from_header_value()` to return the decoded payload.

functions.name: fixlib.jwt.decode_jwt_from_header_value
documentation: Decodes a JWT payload from a http Authorization header value.

functions.name: fixlib.durations.duration_str
documentation:
Convert a timedelta to a string representing the duration human-readable short unit syntax.
Examples: 3d2h, 2min42s
\:param duration: the duration to convert
\:param precision: the number of units to use to represent the duration.
\:param down_to_unit: use all units starting from the biggest one until the given unit.
\:return: string representing the duration

functions.name: fixlib.logger.add_logging_level
documentation:
Comprehensively adds a new logging level to the `logging` module and the
currently configured logging class.

`levelName` becomes an attribute of the `logging` module with the value
`levelNum`. `methodName` becomes a convenience method for both `logging`
itself and the class returned by `logging.getLoggerClass()` (usually just
`logging.Logger`). If `methodName` is not specified, `levelName.lower()` is
used.

To avoid accidental clobberings of existing attributes, this method will
raise an `AttributeError` if the level name is already an attribute of the
`logging` module or if the method name is already present

# Example

```pycon
>>> add_logging_level("TRACE", logging.DEBUG - 5)
>>> logging.getLogger(__name__).setLevel("TRACE")
>>> logging.getLogger(__name__).trace("that worked")
>>> logging.trace("so did this")
>>> logging.TRACE
5
```
via https://stackoverflow.com/a/35804945/92184

classes.name: fixlib.logger.JsonFormatter
documentation:
Simple json log formatter.

methods.name: fixlib.baseresources.BaseResource._keys
documentation:
Return a tuple of all keys that make this resource unique

Must not be called before the resource is connected to the graph
as the relative location within the graph is used to determine the
tuple of unique keys.

E.g. instance -> aws -> 123457 -> us-east-1 -> us-east-1b -> i-987654 -> myServer

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.depth
fixlib.tree.Tree.expand_tree
documentation:
Python generator to traverse the tree (or a subtree) with optional
node filtering and sorting.

Loosely based on an algorithm from 'Essential LISP' by John R. Anderson,
Albert T. Corbett, and Brian J. Reiser, page 239-241.

```
param nid

Node identifier from which tree traversal will start.
If None tree root will be used

param mode

Traversal mode, may be either DEPTH, WIDTH or ZIGZAG

param filter_fn

the @filter function is performed on Node object during
traversing. In this manner, the traversing will NOT visit the node
whose condition does not pass the filter and its children.

param key

the @key and @reverse are present to sort nodes at each
level. If @key is None sorting is performed on node tag.

param reverse

if True reverse sorting

param sorting

if True perform node sorting, if False return
nodes in original insertion order. In latter case @key and
@reverse parameters are ignored.

return

Node IDs that satisfy the conditions

rtype

generator object
```

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.filter_nodes
documentation:
Filters all nodes by function.

```
param func

is passed one node as an argument and that node is included if function returns true,

return

a filter iterator of the node in python 3 or a list of the nodes in python 2.
```

Added by William Rusnack.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.get_node
documentation:
Get the object of the node with ID of `nid`.

An alternative way is using '[]' operation on the tree. But small difference exists between them:
`get_node()` will return None if `nid` is absent, whereas '[]' will raise `KeyError`.

documentation:

Empty list is returned if nid does not exist

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.level
documentation:
Get the node level in this tree.
The level is an integer starting with '0' at the root.
In other words, the root lives at level '0';

Update: @filter params is added to calculate level passing
exclusive nodes.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.link_past_node
documentation:
Delete a node by linking past it.

For example, if we have `a -> b -> c` and delete node b, we are left
with `a -> c`.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.move_node
documentation: Move node @source from its parent to another parent @destination.

```

the node identifier

param grandchild

the node identifier

return

True or False
```

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.nodes
documentation: Return a dict form of nodes in a tree: {id: node_instance}.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.parent
documentation: Get parent {class}`Node` object of given id.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.merge
documentation:
Patch @new_tree on current tree by pasting new_tree root children on current tree @nid node.

Consider the following tree:
# tree.show()
root
├── A
└── B
# new_tree.show()
root2
├── C
└── D
    └── D1
Merging new_tree on B node:
# tree.merge('B', new_tree)
# tree.show()
root
├── A
└── B
    ├── C
    └── D
        └── D1

Note: if current tree is empty and nid is None, the new_tree root will be used as root on current tree. In all
other cases new_tree root is not pasted.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.paste
documentation:
Paste a @new_tree to the original one by linking the root
of new tree to given node (nid).

Update: add @deep copy of pasted tree.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.paths_to_leaves
documentation:
Use this function to get the identifiers allowing to go from the root
nodes to each leaf.

```
return

a list of list of identifiers, root being not omitted.
```

For example:

```python
Harry
|___ Bill
|___ Jane
|    |___ Diane
|         |___ George
|              |___ Jill
|         |___ Mary
|    |___ Mark
```

Expected result:

```python
[['harry', 'jane', 'diane', 'mary'],
 ['harry', 'jane', 'mark'],
 ['harry', 'jane', 'diane', 'george', 'jill'],
 ['harry', 'bill']]
```

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.remove_node
documentation:
Remove a node indicated by 'identifier' with all its successors.
Return the number of removed nodes.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.remove_subtree
documentation:
Get a subtree with `nid` being the root. If nid is None, an
empty tree is returned.

For the original tree, this method is similar to
`remove_node(self,nid)`, because given node and its children
are removed from the original tree in both methods.
For the returned value and performance, these two methods are
different:

> - `remove_node` returns the number of deleted nodes;
> - `remove_subtree` returns a subtree of deleted nodes;

You are always suggested to use `remove_node` if your only to
delete nodes from a tree, as the other one need memory
allocation to store the new tree.

```
return

a  object.
```

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.rsearch
documentation:
Traverse the tree branch along the branch from nid to its
ancestors (until root).

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.siblings
documentation:
Return the siblings of given @nid.

If @nid is root or there are no siblings, an empty list is returned.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.size
documentation:
Get the number of nodes of the whole tree if @level is not
given. Otherwise, the total number of nodes at specific level
is returned.

@param level The level number in the tree. It must be between
[0, tree.depth].

Otherwise, InvalidLevelNumber exception will be raised.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.subtree
documentation:
Return a shallow COPY of subtree with nid being the new root.
If nid is None, return an empty tree.
If you are looking for a deepcopy, please create a new tree
with this shallow copy, e.g.,

```python
new_tree = Tree(t.subtree(t.root), deep=True)
```

This line creates a deep copy of the entire tree.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.update_node
documentation:
Update node's attributes.

```
param nid

the identifier of modified node

param attrs

attribute pairs recognized by Node object

return

None
```

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.to_dict
documentation: Transform the whole tree into a dict.

methods.name: fixlib.tree.Tree.to_json
documentation: To format the tree in JSON format.

classes.name: fixlib.graph.Graph

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.merge
documentation:
Merge another graph into ourselves

If the other graph has a graph.root an edge will be created between
it and our own graph root.

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.add_resource
documentation:
Add a resource node to the graph

When adding resource nodes to the graph there's always a label and a
kind as well as an edge connecting the new resource with its parent
resource. This way we should never have disconnected nodes within the graph.

The graph_attributes are a Dict of key=value pairs that contain all the
attributes of a graph node. When working with the graph that information isn't
too important as each node is a standard Python object containing all its
properties. However when exporting in graphml, gexf or json those attributes are
the only thing being exported with the node name. So we'll try to turn every
public property of a graph node into key=value attribute pairs to be exported
with the graph.

Attributes can also be used in graph searches to find nodes matching certain
attributes.

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.is_acyclic_per_edge_type
documentation:
Checks if the graph is acyclic with respect to each edge type.
This means it is valid if there are cycles in the graph but not for the same edge type.
\:return: True if the graph is acyclic for all edge types, otherwise False.

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.find_cycle
documentation:
Returns the first cycle found in the graph per edge type.
\:return: first cycle found in the graph per edge type, or None if no cycles are found.

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.search
documentation: Search for graph nodes by their attribute value

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.searchre
documentation: Regex search for graph nodes by their attribute value

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.searchall
documentation: Search for graph nodes by multiple attributes and values

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.search_first
documentation: Return the first graph node that matches a certain attribute value

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.search_first_all
documentation: Return the first graph node that matches multiple attributes and values

methods.name: fixlib.graph.Graph.search_first_parent_class
documentation:
Return the first parent node matching a certain class

This is being used to search up the graph and e.g. find the account that the
graph node is a member of.
A directed Graph

classes.name: fixlib.core.CLIEnvelope
documentation:
Envelope fields that are used by the CLI.
Those fields are encoded as HTTP Headers into the HTTP response.

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.optional_origin
documentation: List[X] -> list, list -> list

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.is_optional
documentation: Optional[x] -> true

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.is_collection
documentation: List[x] -> true, list -> true

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.is_dict
documentation: Dict[x,y] -> true, dict -> true

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.is_enum
documentation: either enum or optional enum

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.type_arg
documentation: List[X] -> X, list -> object

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.dict_types
documentation: Dict[X,Y] -> (X,Y), dict -> (object, object)

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.transitive_classes
documentation: walk class hierarchy, as well as all properties to find transitive data classes

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.model_name
documentation: Model name from the internal python class name

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.should_export
documentation: define if a field should be exported or not.
Use python default: hide props starting with underscore.

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.dataclasses_to_fixcore_model
documentation:
Analyze all transitive dataclasses and create the model
definition as understood by fixcore.
A plain python dataclass defines the model structure and
should be used to create json in the same format.

```
param classes

all dataclasses to analyze.

param allow_unknown_props

allow properties in json that are not defined in the model.

param aggregate_root

if a type is a subtype of this type, it will be considered an aggregate root.

param walk_subclasses

if true, all subclasses of the given classes will be analyzed as well.

param use_optional_as_required

if true, all non-optional fields will be considered required.

param with_kind_description

if true, include the description for classes.

param with_prop_description

if true, include the description for properties.

return

the model definition in the fixcore json format.
```

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.dynamic_object_to_fixcore_model
documentation: Use this model exporter, if a dynamic object is exported
with given name and properties.

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_export.node_from_dict
documentation:
Create a resource from fixcore graph node data

If include_select_ancestors is True, the resource will be created with
ancestors cloud, account, region and zone passed directly to the constructor.
This is useful for when the node is being created for standalone use instead
of in the context of a graph. E.g. during tagging of an individual resource
where the tagger needs to know which cloud and account a resource came from.

functions.name: fixlib.core.model_check.check_overlap
documentation:
Call this method from your collector plugin to check for overlapping properties.
This will try to load all models from all known plugins.
The call will fail if the imports are not working - make sure the calling side has all those plugins installed.

@param base: additional base classes to check for overlapping properties. All existing known plugins are added.
:raise Exception: if there is an overlap

classes.name: fixlib.core.model_check.CheckProp
documentation: fmt: on

classes.name: fixlib.core.custom_command.CommandDefinition
documentation:
A final decorator is called by the interpreter once after the class is defined.
(Not the case for decorators with attributes)
This decorator will maintain all decorated methods on the class side.
Use task_definitions(clazz) to get all task definitions of a specific class.

classes.name: fixlib.core.custom_command.execute_command
documentation:
In case you want to expose a method as worker task to the core, you can use this decorator.
The definition is used to register a custom command at the core, when the worker connects.
The custom command can be executed with the provided name.
Note: any existing command or alias takes precedence over the custom command.

The expected signature of the function is this:

def some_name(self, config: Config, js: Json, args: List[str]) -> JsonElement:

Notes on the signature:
\- the json is the node section of the core task data.
\- the args contains the list of arguments provided by the user.
\- the function returns json. None is a valid json element.

```
param name

the name of the command

param info

a short description of the command

param args_description

a dictionary of argument names and their description

param description

a longer description of the command

param expect_node_result

if true, the returned json is a resource and the current node in the db will be updated.

param allowed_on_kind

if set, the command will only be executed on resources of this kind

param filter

filter attributes as selector for this command.
Multiple workers might register the same command on the same resource with different filters.
Based on the resource the task is delegated to the worker with the matching filter.

return

the decorated function that will be registered as worker task.
```
noinspection PyPep8Naming

classes.name: fixlib.core.custom_command.execute_command_on_resource
documentation:
See execute_command.

This decorator will convert the incoming message to a resource object
and a resulting object back to the json representation.

The expected signature of the function is this:

def some_name(

: self, config: Config, resource: Optional[BaseResource], args: List[str]

) -> Union\[JsonElement, BaseResource\]:

Notes on the signature:
\- the resource is marked as optional. In case the command is executed in source position, no resource is provided.
\- the args contains the list of arguments provided by the user.
\- the function can decide to return either a json element or a resource. None is a valid json element.

```
param name

the name of the command

param info

a short description of the command

param args_description

a dictionary of argument names and their description

param description

a longer description of the command

param expect_node_result

if true, the returned resource will update the current node in the database.

param allowed_on_kind

if set, the command will only be executed on resources of this kind

param filter

filter attributes as selector for this command.
Multiple workers might register the same command on the same resource with different filters.
Based on the resource the task is delegated to the worker with the matching filter.

return

the decorated function that will be registered as worker task.
```
noinspection PyPep8Naming

classes.name: fixlib.lock.RWLock
documentation:
Synchronization object used in a solution of so-called second
readers-writers problem. In this problem, many readers can simultaneously
access a share, and a writer has an exclusive access to this share.
Additionally, the following constraints should be met:
1) no reader should be kept waiting if the share is currently opened for
    reading unless a writer is also waiting for the share,
2) no writer should be kept waiting for the share longer than absolutely
    necessary.

The implementation is based on [1, secs. 4.2.2, 4.2.6, 4.2.7]
with a modification -- adding an additional lock (C{self.__readers_queue})
-- in accordance with [2].

Sources:
[1] A.B. Downey: "The little book of semaphores", Version 2.1.5, 2008
[2] P.J. Courtois, F. Heymans, D.L. Parnas:
    "Concurrent Control with 'Readers' and 'Writers'",
    Communications of the ACM, 1971 (via [3])
[3] http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Readers-writers_problem

This code is a derivative of the code from ActiveState Code service at: http://
code.activestate.com/recipes/577803-reader-writer-lock-with-priority-for-writers
and is licensed under the MIT license.

classes.name: fixlib.lock._LightSwitch
documentation:
An auxiliary "light switch"-like object. The first thread turns on the
"switch", the last one turns it off (see [1, sec. 4.2.2] for details).

classes.name: fixlib.baseplugin.PluginType
documentation:
Defines Plugin Type

COLLECTOR is a cloud resource collector plugin that gets instantiated
on each collect() run
PERSISTENT is a persistent plugin that gets instantiated once upon startup

classes.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BasePlugin

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BasePlugin.go
documentation: Do the Plugin work

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BasePlugin.add_args
documentation: Adds Plugin specific arguments to the global arg parser

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BasePlugin.add_config
documentation: Adds Plugin specific config options

A fix Plugin is a thread that does some work.

If the plugin_type is PluginType.COLLECTOR the Plugin gets instantiated each
collect run.
If the plugin_type is PluginType.PERSISTENT the Plugin gets instantiated upon
startup and is expected to run forever. It may register to any events it's
interested in and act upon them.

Upon start the go() method is called. For COLLECTOR Plugins collect() is called.

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseActionPlugin.do_action
documentation: Perform an action

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseActionPlugin.action_processor
documentation: Process incoming action messages

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseActionPlugin.bootstrap
documentation:
Bootstrap the plugin.

If bootstrapping is successful the plugin is ready to run.

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseActionPlugin.add_args
documentation: Adds Plugin specific arguments to the global arg parser

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseActionPlugin.add_config
documentation: Adds Plugin specific config options

class_variables.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseActionPlugin.action
documentation: Name of the action this plugin implements

classes.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin.collect
documentation: Collects all the Cloud Resources

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin.auto_enableable
documentation: Should this collector be enabled by default?

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin.update_tag
documentation: Update the tag of a resource

methods.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin.delete_tag
documentation: Delete the tag of a resource

class_variables.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin.plugin_type
documentation: Type of the Plugin

class_variables.name: fixlib.baseplugin.BaseCollectorPlugin.cloud
documentation: Name of the cloud this plugin implements

A fix Collector plugin is a thread that collects cloud resources.

Whenever the thread is started the collect() method is run. The collect() method
is expected to add cloud resources to self.graph. Cloud resources must inherit
the BaseResource or one of the more specific resource types like BaseAccount,
BaseInstance, BaseNetwork, BaseLoadBalancer, etc.

When the collect() method finishes, the Collector will retrieve the
Plugins Graph and append it to the global Graph.

documentation: Note: the config is mutable.

documentation: Initialize the global config.

methods.name: fixlib.config.Config.add_config

Add a config to the config manager.

Takes a dataclass as input and adds its fields to the config store.
Dataclass must have a kind ClassVar which specifies the top level config name.
